1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying," The witnesses note the ordinance of the red heifer now given, for the cleansing of those defiled by the dead. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was spoken, as Aben Ezra observes, in the wilderness of Sinai, when the Lord commanded unclean persons be put out of the camp and some were defiled by a dead body. The witnesses note that Henry heads it the divine appointment for the burning of a red heifer to ashes, and the preserving of the ashes for purification. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron; the for the defiled — those polluted by the dead; the the red heifer — to be burnt to ashes for purifying; the a standing remedy — cleansing always ready at hand.

The witnesses agree the law provided a standing remedy for the commonest defilement, the touch of death: in a people who would daily meet with the dead, God appointed the ashes of a heifer, kept ready, by which the unclean might be cleansed; the very ordinance bespeaks his care that his dwelling among them not be defiled, and his provision that sin's defilement, here figured by death, should always have a cleansing at hand.

So the witnesses make the verse the ordinance given: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron. The witnesses note the for the defiled: those polluted by the dead. The witnesses note the the red heifer: to be burnt to ashes for purifying. The witnesses note the a standing remedy: cleansing always ready at hand. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron. The expositors observe the for the defiled: those polluted by the dead. The expositors observe the the red heifer: to be burnt to ashes for purifying. The expositors observe the a standing remedy: cleansing always ready at hand. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the for the defiled, the the red heifer, the a standing remedy, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that God gave the ordinance of the red heifer — a standing remedy for the defilement of death, a cleansing always kept ready for his people.
2:
"This is the ordinance of the law which the LORD hath commanded, saying, Speak unto the children of Israel, that they bring thee a red heifer without spot, wherein is no blemish, and upon which never came yoke:" The witnesses note the heifer — red, without spot or blemish, never yoked — appointed for the purification. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was a ceremonial ordinance, founded not on plain reason in the thing but in the will of God, intended as a type and shadow of the blood and sacrifice of Christ and the efficacy of that to cleanse from sin. The witnesses note that the heifer was to be red, without blemish, and never to have borne a yoke. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the red — the colour of blood and of the sin he bore; the without spot or blemish — the unblemished victim, as the Lamb; the never came yoke — never under sin's yoke, offering freely; the a type of Christ — the shadow of his cleansing sacrifice (Heb. 9:13-14).

The reformed reader (Gill) opens the type at once: the heifer answers to Christ in every mark — red, the colour of blood and of sin he bore; without spot or blemish, as the Lamb without blemish; never yoked, as Christ was never under the yoke of sin or constraint, offering himself freely; the whole rite a shadow of his sacrifice, whose blood cleanses where this could only figure (Heb. 9:13-14).

So the witnesses make the verse the red heifer: The witnesses note the red: the colour of blood and of the sin he bore. The witnesses note the without spot or blemish: the unblemished victim, as the Lamb. The witnesses note the never came yoke: never under sin's yoke, offering freely. The witnesses note the a type of Christ: the shadow of his cleansing sacrifice (Heb. 9:13-14). The expositors observe the red: the colour of blood and of the sin he bore. The expositors observe the without spot or blemish: the unblemished victim, as the Lamb. The expositors observe the never came yoke: never under sin's yoke, offering freely. The expositors observe the a type of Christ: the shadow of his cleansing sacrifice (Heb. 9:13-14). The believer is taught to mark the red, the without spot or blemish, the never came yoke, the a type of Christ, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the red heifer, without blemish and never yoked, was appointed — a type of Christ in every mark, the shadow of his blood that cleanses from sin (Heb. 9:13-14).
3:
"And ye shall give her unto Eleazar the priest, that he may bring her forth without the camp, and one shall slay her before his face:" The witnesses note the heifer given to Eleazar, brought outside the camp, and slain in his sight. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was given to Eleazar, not Aaron, that the high priest might not be defiled and hindered in his office, and to inure Eleazar to his work. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the sacrifice was superintended by Eleazar, brought without the camp and slain before his face. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the given to Eleazar — that the high priest be not defiled; the without the camp — the victim led beyond the holy place; the slain before his face — killed in the priest's sight; the the type — jesus suffering without the gate (Heb. 13:12).

The witnesses agree the slaying outside the camp is one of the clearest pointings to Christ: as the heifer was led beyond the camp and there killed, so Jesus, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate (Heb. 13:12); the rite removed the sin-bearing victim from the holy place to the place of reproach, foreshadowing the Lord crucified outside Jerusalem, bearing the defilement away from the camp of God.

So the witnesses make the verse slain without the camp: The witnesses note the given to Eleazar: that the high priest be not defiled. The witnesses note the without the camp: the victim led beyond the holy place. The witnesses note the slain before his face: killed in the priest's sight. The witnesses note the the type: Jesus suffering without the gate (Heb. 13:12). The expositors observe the given to Eleazar: that the high priest be not defiled. The expositors observe the without the camp: the victim led beyond the holy place. The expositors observe the slain before his face: killed in the priest's sight. The expositors observe the the type: Jesus suffering without the gate (Heb. 13:12). The believer is taught to mark the given to Eleazar, the without the camp, the slain before his face, the the type, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the heifer was brought outside the camp and slain — a clear type of Christ, who suffered without the gate to sanctify his people with his own blood (Heb. 13:12).
4:
"And Eleazar the priest shall take of her blood with his finger, and sprinkle of her blood directly before the tabernacle of the congregation seven times:" The witnesses note the blood sprinkled with the finger toward the tabernacle seven times — the perfect application of atoning blood. The witnesses note that Gill notes the blood was taken in the hand and sprinkled with the finger toward the tabernacle, and cites Ainsworth that the finger signified the Spirit of Christ, the "finger of God," who takes Christ's blood and sprinkles it on the hearts of his people, freeing them from an evil conscience. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the sevenfold sprinkling before the tabernacle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the with his finger — the finger a figure of the Spirit, the finger of God; the before the tabernacle — the blood presented toward the sanctuary; the seven times — the perfect, complete sprinkling; the applied to the heart — the Spirit sprinkling Christ's blood on the conscience (Heb. 9:14).

The witnesses agree the blood, though the heifer was slain outside, was directed toward the sanctuary: the seven sprinklings, the number of completeness, presented the atoning blood before God, that the sacrifice be accepted; Gill, with Ainsworth, draws the gospel — the priest's finger a figure of the Spirit, the "finger of God," who applies the blood of Christ to the heart, sprinkling the conscience clean from dead works (Heb. 9:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the sprinkled blood: The witnesses note the with his finger: the finger a figure of the Spirit, the finger of God. The witnesses note the before the tabernacle: the blood presented toward the sanctuary. The witnesses note the seven times: the perfect, complete sprinkling. The witnesses note the applied to the heart: the Spirit sprinkling Christ's blood on the conscience (Heb. 9:14). The expositors observe the with his finger: the finger a figure of the Spirit, the finger of God. The expositors observe the before the tabernacle: the blood presented toward the sanctuary. The expositors observe the seven times: the perfect, complete sprinkling. The expositors observe the applied to the heart: the Spirit sprinkling Christ's blood on the conscience (Heb. 9:14). The believer is taught to mark the with his finger, the before the tabernacle, the seven times, the applied to the heart, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the blood was sprinkled toward the tabernacle seven times — the perfect presentation of atoning blood, the finger a figure of the Spirit who applies Christ's blood to the conscience (Heb. 9:14).
5:
"And one shall burn the heifer in his sight; her skin, and her flesh, and her blood, with her dung, shall he burn:" The witnesses note the whole heifer — skin, flesh, blood, and dung — burned entirely in the priest's sight. The witnesses note that Gill relays the rabbinic account of the burning at the mount of Olives, the cow bound and laid on a pile of cedar, the priest looking on. The witnesses note that the whole creature, nothing reserved, was consumed by fire. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the burn the heifer — the creature given wholly to the fire; the skin, flesh, and blood — no part reserved; the with her dung — even the basest consumed; the the whole self-offering — christ given entirely for his people.

The witnesses agree the entire burning marks the completeness of the sacrifice: not a part but the whole heifer — even the blood and the dung — was given to the fire, that nothing of the victim remained but the ashes; the total consuming figures the whole self-offering of Christ, who gave himself entirely, body and soul, a sacrifice wholly consumed in the fire of God's judgment for the cleansing of his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole burnt: The witnesses note the burn the heifer: the creature given wholly to the fire. The witnesses note the skin, flesh, and blood: no part reserved. The witnesses note the with her dung: even the basest consumed. The witnesses note the the whole self-offering: Christ given entirely for his people. The expositors observe the burn the heifer: the creature given wholly to the fire. The expositors observe the skin, flesh, and blood: no part reserved. The expositors observe the with her dung: even the basest consumed. The expositors observe the the whole self-offering: Christ given entirely for his people. The believer is taught to mark the burn the heifer, the skin, flesh, and blood, the with her dung, the the whole self-offering, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the whole heifer was burned, nothing reserved — the completeness of the sacrifice, figuring the entire self-offering of Christ wholly consumed for his people's cleansing.
6:
"And the priest shall take cedar wood, and hyssop, and scarlet, and cast it into the midst of the burning of the heifer." The witnesses note the cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet cast into the fire with the heifer — the same as at the cleansing of the leper. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were the same three things used at the cleansing of the leper (Lev. 14:4). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note them cast into the midst of the burning. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cedar wood — durable and of sweet savour; the hyssop — the lowly herb of sprinkling (Ps. 51:7); the scarlet — the colour of blood; the as at the leper's cleansing — the same emblems of purifying (Lev. 14:4).

The witnesses agree the three ingredients, joined to the leper's rite, gathered up the same emblems of cleansing into the heifer's ashes: the cedar (durable, of sweet savour), the lowly hyssop (the herb of sprinkling, Ps. 51:7), and the scarlet (the colour of blood) were burned with the heifer, that their virtue too might pass into the ashes — the whole pointing to the cleansing power of Christ's blood, in which the proud is humbled and the guilty washed white.

So the witnesses make the verse cedar, hyssop, scarlet: The witnesses note the cedar wood: durable and of sweet savour. The witnesses note the hyssop: the lowly herb of sprinkling (Ps. 51:7). The witnesses note the scarlet: the colour of blood. The witnesses note the as at the leper's cleansing: the same emblems of purifying (Lev. 14:4). The expositors observe the cedar wood: durable and of sweet savour. The expositors observe the hyssop: the lowly herb of sprinkling (Ps. 51:7). The expositors observe the scarlet: the colour of blood. The expositors observe the as at the leper's cleansing: the same emblems of purifying (Lev. 14:4). The believer is taught to mark the cedar wood, the hyssop, the scarlet, the as at the leper's cleansing, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that cedar, hyssop, and scarlet were cast into the burning — the emblems of cleansing from the leper's rite gathered into the ashes, pointing to the purifying power of Christ's blood (Ps. 51:7).
7:
"Then the priest shall wash his clothes, and he shall bathe his flesh in water, and afterward he shall come into the camp, and the priest shall be unclean until the even." The witnesses note the strange paradox — the priest who prepared the purifying ashes was himself made unclean until evening. The witnesses note that Gill notes all who were employed about the heifer, from first to last, were defiled in their garments, the creature reckoned so impure though its ashes were for purifying. The witnesses note that even Eleazar, who sprinkled the blood, was unclean and needed washing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priest wash his clothes — the minister himself defiled; the unclean until even — made impure by handling the heifer; the the paradox — the rite that cleanses defiles its ministers; the the type — christ made sin, defiled to cleanse us (2 Cor. 5:21).

The witnesses agree the paradox is full of gospel mystery: the very rite that cleansed the unclean defiled the clean who handled it, the heifer that purified others made its ministers impure; so Christ, who cleanses his people, was himself "made sin for us" (2 Cor. 5:21) and numbered with transgressors, taking on himself the defilement he bore away — the sin-bearer reckoned unclean that the guilty might be made clean.

So the witnesses make the verse the defiling purifier: The witnesses note the the priest wash his clothes: the minister himself defiled. The witnesses note the unclean until even: made impure by handling the heifer. The witnesses note the the paradox: the rite that cleanses defiles its ministers. The witnesses note the the type: Christ made sin, defiled to cleanse us (2 Cor. 5:21). The expositors observe the the priest wash his clothes: the minister himself defiled. The expositors observe the unclean until even: made impure by handling the heifer. The expositors observe the the paradox: the rite that cleanses defiles its ministers. The expositors observe the the type: Christ made sin, defiled to cleanse us (2 Cor. 5:21). The believer is taught to mark the the priest wash his clothes, the unclean until even, the the paradox, the the type, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the priest who prepared the ashes was himself made unclean — the paradox figuring Christ, who was made sin that we might be made clean, the sin-bearer defiled to cleanse the guilty (2 Cor. 5:21).
8:
"And he that burneth her shall wash his clothes in water, and bathe his flesh in water, and shall be unclean until the even." The witnesses note that he who burned the heifer was likewise defiled and must wash, unclean until evening. The witnesses note that Gill notes this shows one distinct from him that sprinkled the blood, all concerned being defiled. The witnesses note that he too washed his clothes and bathed his flesh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he that burneth her — the second minister defiled; the wash and bathe — cleansing required of him too; the unclean until even — impure for handling the heifer; the every hand defiled — the cost borne by all who served the rite.

The witnesses agree the same defilement fell on every hand that touched the work: not the sprinkler only but the burner, and afterward the gatherer of the ashes, were each made unclean; the repetition presses the lesson that the means of purification was itself reckoned defiling to all who served it, every minister of the cleansing bearing for the moment the uncleanness of the sin it dealt with — a constant witness to the cost of dealing with defilement.

So the witnesses make the verse the defiled burner: The witnesses note the he that burneth her: the second minister defiled. The witnesses note the wash and bathe: cleansing required of him too. The witnesses note the unclean until even: impure for handling the heifer. The witnesses note the every hand defiled: the cost borne by all who served the rite. The expositors observe the he that burneth her: the second minister defiled. The expositors observe the wash and bathe: cleansing required of him too. The expositors observe the unclean until even: impure for handling the heifer. The expositors observe the every hand defiled: the cost borne by all who served the rite. The believer is taught to mark the he that burneth her, the wash and bathe, the unclean until even, the every hand defiled, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the burner of the heifer was also defiled and must wash — every minister of the cleansing made unclean, a witness to the cost of dealing with defilement.
9:
"And a man that is clean shall gather up the ashes of the heifer, and lay them up without the camp in a clean place... for a water of separation: it is a purification for sin." The witnesses note the ashes gathered by a clean man and kept outside the camp for the water of separation — a purification for sin. The witnesses note that Gill relays the great care taken in later times that the priest who burned the cow be pure, separated seven days, and a causeway built over Kidron lest any unseen grave defile. The witnesses note that the ashes were laid up and kept for the congregation as a water of separation, a purification for sin. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a clean man gather — the ashes carefully preserved; the without the camp — laid up in a clean place; the a water of separation — kept ready for the whole congregation; the a purification for sin — one sacrifice of abiding efficacy (Heb. 10:14).

The witnesses agree the preserving of the ashes is rich in meaning: the heifer was burned but once, yet its ashes, kept ready, served the whole congregation for all generations — one sacrifice of abiding and ever-available efficacy; the figure points to Christ, offered once, whose one sacrifice avails for his people through all time, the virtue of his blood ever fresh to cleanse the defiled who come to it (Heb. 10:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the kept ashes: The witnesses note the a clean man gather: the ashes carefully preserved. The witnesses note the without the camp: laid up in a clean place. The witnesses note the a water of separation: kept ready for the whole congregation. The witnesses note the a purification for sin: one sacrifice of abiding efficacy (Heb. 10:14). The expositors observe the a clean man gather: the ashes carefully preserved. The expositors observe the without the camp: laid up in a clean place. The expositors observe the a water of separation: kept ready for the whole congregation. The expositors observe the a purification for sin: one sacrifice of abiding efficacy (Heb. 10:14). The believer is taught to mark the a clean man gather, the without the camp, the a water of separation, the a purification for sin, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the ashes were kept for the water of separation — one sacrifice of abiding efficacy, a figure of Christ offered once, his blood ever fresh to cleanse the defiled (Heb. 10:14).
10:
"And he that gathereth the ashes of the heifer shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even: and it shall be unto the children of Israel, and unto the stranger that sojourneth among them, for a statute for ever." The witnesses note the gatherer too defiled, and the statute given for Israel and the stranger alike, for ever. The witnesses note that Gill notes even the gatherer of the ashes was defiled and must wash. The witnesses note that Ellicott sees in the statute reaching the stranger a figure that the remission of sins through Christ was not to the Jews and their children only, but to all afar off (Acts 2:39). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the gatherer defiled — even he made unclean and washing; the for the children of Israel — the cleansing for the covenant people; the and the stranger — the sojourner included alike; the the gospel breadth — cleansing in Christ to all afar off (Acts 2:39).

The witnesses agree the cleansing was offered on equal terms to all within the covenant, native and stranger: the water of separation availed for every defiled person who would use it, the foreigner sojourning among them included; the grammatical reader draws the gospel breadth — the promise of cleansing in Christ reaches not Israel only but all that are afar off, as many as the Lord shall call (Acts 2:39), the door of purification open to all.

So the witnesses make the verse the statute for all: The witnesses note the the gatherer defiled: even he made unclean and washing. The witnesses note the for the children of Israel: the cleansing for the covenant people. The witnesses note the and the stranger: the sojourner included alike. The witnesses note the the gospel breadth: cleansing in Christ to all afar off (Acts 2:39). The expositors observe the the gatherer defiled: even he made unclean and washing. The expositors observe the for the children of Israel: the cleansing for the covenant people. The expositors observe the and the stranger: the sojourner included alike. The expositors observe the the gospel breadth: cleansing in Christ to all afar off (Acts 2:39). The believer is taught to mark the the gatherer defiled, the for the children of Israel, the and the stranger, the the gospel breadth, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the statute reached Israel and the stranger alike — the cleansing offered to all, a figure of the remission in Christ that reaches all that are afar off (Acts 2:39).
11:
"He that toucheth the dead body of any man shall be unclean seven days." The witnesses note the chief defilement the water of separation cleansed — the touch of a dead body, making unclean seven days. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is instanced as the principal pollution for which the water of purification was appointed; touching a dead beast defiled only till evening, but a dead man seven days. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the seven days' uncleanness on whoever touched a corpse. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the toucheth the dead — the chief defilement named; the unclean seven days — the longest of common uncleannesses; the death the emblem of sin — the wages of sin, spiritual death; the cleansed by the water — pointing to the blood that purges dead works (Heb. 9:14).

The witnesses agree death is named as the defilement above all others, for it is the wages and the very emblem of sin: contact with a dead body, the longest and gravest of common uncleannesses, fitly stands for the pollution of sin, which is spiritual death; the water of separation that alone could cleanse it points to the blood of Christ, which alone purges the conscience from dead works and the defilement of sin and death (Heb. 9:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the touch of death: The witnesses note the toucheth the dead: the chief defilement named. The witnesses note the unclean seven days: the longest of common uncleannesses. The witnesses note the death the emblem of sin: the wages of sin, spiritual death. The witnesses note the cleansed by the water: pointing to the blood that purges dead works (Heb. 9:14). The expositors observe the toucheth the dead: the chief defilement named. The expositors observe the unclean seven days: the longest of common uncleannesses. The expositors observe the death the emblem of sin: the wages of sin, spiritual death. The expositors observe the cleansed by the water: pointing to the blood that purges dead works (Heb. 9:14). The believer is taught to mark the toucheth the dead, the unclean seven days, the death the emblem of sin, the cleansed by the water, and to look to the blood that cleanses. The verse thus records that the touch of the dead defiled seven days — death the chief emblem of sin's pollution, cleansed by the water that figures the blood of Christ purging dead works (Heb. 9:14).
12:
"He shall purify himself with it on the third day, and on the seventh day he shall be clean: but if he purify not himself the third day, then the seventh day he shall not be clean." The witnesses note the appointed days of purifying — the third and the seventh — and that to neglect the third spoiled the whole. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was to purify himself with the water of purification, first on the third day. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rule — purified on the third and seventh, but if not on the third, then not clean on the seventh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the third day — the first sprinkling, as needful as the last; the the seventh day — the completing of the cleansing; the if not the third — the whole purification spoiled; the God's way and time — the cleansing not to be taken by halves.

The witnesses agree the fixed order taught that God's cleansing is to be used in his own way and time: the sprinkling on the third day was as needful as on the seventh, and to omit it left the man unclean however he kept the rest; the lesson is that the means of grace cannot be taken by halves nor on our own terms — God's appointed method must be followed entire, or the cleansing is not had.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointed days: The witnesses note the the third day: the first sprinkling, as needful as the last. The witnesses note the the seventh day: the completing of the cleansing. The witnesses note the if not the third: the whole purification spoiled. The witnesses note the God's way and time: the cleansing not to be taken by halves. The expositors observe the the third day: the first sprinkling, as needful as the last. The expositors observe the the seventh day: the completing of the cleansing. The expositors observe the if not the third: the whole purification spoiled. The expositors observe the God's way and time: the cleansing not to be taken by halves. The believer is taught to mark the the third day, the the seventh day, the if not the third, the God's way and time, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that he was purified on the third and seventh days, the third as needful as the last — God's cleansing to be used in his own way and time, not taken by halves.
13:
"Whosoever toucheth the dead body of any man that is dead, and purifieth not himself, defileth the tabernacle of the LORD; and that soul shall be cut off from Israel..." The witnesses note the grave penalty — the unpurified defiles the tabernacle and is cut off from Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of the man who neglects the means God appointed for his cleansing. The witnesses note that Calvin marks how pleasing purity is to God: a forgotten sprinkling might be made up by a later day, but to enter the sanctuary unclean was a capital crime, mixing holy and profane. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the purifieth not himself — neglecting the appointed cleansing; the defileth the tabernacle — bringing uncleanness into God's house; the cut off from Israel — the grave penalty of neglect; the purity dear to God — holy and profane not to be mixed.

The reformed reader (Calvin) presses the severity's lesson: the heavy penalty shows how dear purity is to God and how grave it is to bring uncleanness into his presence; mere delay in purifying might be remedied, but to enter the sanctuary defiled, confounding the holy with the unclean, was a sin punished by being cut off — a solemn warning that those who slight God's cleansing and yet press into his presence profane his house and forfeit their place among his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the unpurified cut off: The witnesses note the purifieth not himself: neglecting the appointed cleansing. The witnesses note the defileth the tabernacle: bringing uncleanness into God's house. The witnesses note the cut off from Israel: the grave penalty of neglect. The witnesses note the purity dear to God: holy and profane not to be mixed. The expositors observe the purifieth not himself: neglecting the appointed cleansing. The expositors observe the defileth the tabernacle: bringing uncleanness into God's house. The expositors observe the cut off from Israel: the grave penalty of neglect. The expositors observe the purity dear to God: holy and profane not to be mixed. The believer is taught to mark the purifieth not himself, the defileth the tabernacle, the cut off from Israel, the purity dear to God, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that the unpurified defiled the tabernacle and was cut off — the severity showing how dear purity is to God, the holy not to be confounded with the unclean.
14:
"This is the law, when a man dieth in a tent: all that come into the tent, and all that is in the tent, shall be unclean seven days." The witnesses note the law of death in a tent — all who enter and all that is within made unclean seven days. The witnesses note that Gill notes a tent is named because the Israelites then dwelt in tents, but the law holds equally for a house. The witnesses note that all who came in and all that was in it were defiled. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a man dieth in a tent — the place of death named; the all that come in — every person entering defiled; the all that is in the tent — the whole dwelling polluted; the the spread of defilement — the contagiousness of death and sin.

The witnesses agree the reach of the defilement teaches how pervasive is the pollution of death: not the body only but the whole dwelling and all in it, and every person who entered, were made unclean — sin and death spreading their taint over all they touch; the law, by making the very air of the house of death defiling, impressed on Israel the contagiousness of corruption, and the need of a cleansing wide enough to reach the whole.

So the witnesses make the verse death in the tent: The witnesses note the a man dieth in a tent: the place of death named. The witnesses note the all that come in: every person entering defiled. The witnesses note the all that is in the tent: the whole dwelling polluted. The witnesses note the the spread of defilement: the contagiousness of death and sin. The expositors observe the a man dieth in a tent: the place of death named. The expositors observe the all that come in: every person entering defiled. The expositors observe the all that is in the tent: the whole dwelling polluted. The expositors observe the the spread of defilement: the contagiousness of death and sin. The believer is taught to mark the a man dieth in a tent, the all that come in, the all that is in the tent, the the spread of defilement, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that death in a tent defiled all within and all who entered — the pervasiveness of the pollution of death, the contagiousness of corruption needing a wide cleansing.
15:
"And every open vessel, which hath no covering bound upon it, is unclean." The witnesses note that an open vessel in the tent of death, having no cover bound on, was made unclean. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi and Maimonides, notes this is to be understood of an earthen vessel. The witnesses note that the vessel uncovered took the defilement, while a covered one was preserved. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the every open vessel — the earthen vessel uncovered; the no covering bound — left open to the defilement; the is unclean — taking the taint of death; the the guarded heart — the covered vessel kept, the open defiled.

The witnesses agree the small detail carries a quiet lesson: the open vessel was defiled, but the one tightly covered escaped the pollution; so the heart left open and unguarded takes the taint of the death around it, while that which is sealed and watched is kept — a figure that to be preserved from the contagion of sin one must keep the heart with all diligence, covered and shut against the defilement that spreads where there is no guard.

So the witnesses make the verse the open vessel: The witnesses note the every open vessel: the earthen vessel uncovered. The witnesses note the no covering bound: left open to the defilement. The witnesses note the is unclean: taking the taint of death. The witnesses note the the guarded heart: the covered vessel kept, the open defiled. The expositors observe the every open vessel: the earthen vessel uncovered. The expositors observe the no covering bound: left open to the defilement. The expositors observe the is unclean: taking the taint of death. The expositors observe the the guarded heart: the covered vessel kept, the open defiled. The believer is taught to mark the every open vessel, the no covering bound, the is unclean, the the guarded heart, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that the open vessel was defiled while the covered escaped — a figure that the unguarded heart takes the taint of sin, but that which is watched and sealed is kept.
16:
"And whosoever toucheth one that is slain with a sword in the open fields, or a dead body, or a bone of a man, or a grave, shall be unclean seven days." The witnesses note the defilement extended to every contact with death — the slain, the dead, a bone, or a grave. The witnesses note that Gill notes one slain by the sword or any violent death, or one dead a natural death, or even a bone or a grave, defiled. The witnesses note that the touch of any of these made unclean seven days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one slain — the violently killed defiling; the a dead body — the naturally dead also; the a bone or a grave — even the least remains of death; the unclean seven days — death's pollution clinging to all its traces.

The witnesses agree the law left no contact with death outside its reach: whether a man slain in the field, a body, a single bone, or even a grave unknowingly touched, all conveyed the seven days' uncleanness; the very thoroughness shows that death's pollution clings to its least remains, and that sin, which death betokens, defiles in all its traces — so that a man might be made unclean by what he never saw, as sin's contagion reaches further than we know.

So the witnesses make the verse every touch of death: The witnesses note the one slain: the violently killed defiling. The witnesses note the a dead body: the naturally dead also. The witnesses note the a bone or a grave: even the least remains of death. The witnesses note the unclean seven days: death's pollution clinging to all its traces. The expositors observe the one slain: the violently killed defiling. The expositors observe the a dead body: the naturally dead also. The expositors observe the a bone or a grave: even the least remains of death. The expositors observe the unclean seven days: death's pollution clinging to all its traces. The believer is taught to mark the one slain, the a dead body, the a bone or a grave, the unclean seven days, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that every contact with death — the slain, a body, a bone, a grave — defiled seven days; death's pollution clinging to its least remains, as sin's contagion reaches further than we know.
17:
"And for an unclean person they shall take of the ashes of the burnt heifer of purification for sin, and running water shall be put thereto in a vessel:" The witnesses note the making of the water of separation — the ashes of the heifer with running water put together in a vessel. The witnesses note that Gill reads it of the ashes of the burnt sin-offering, and Ellicott renders it "the burning of the sin-offering." The witnesses note that Wesley reads the running water (from a spring or river, the purest) as signifying God's Spirit, by which alone true purification is obtained — those who promise themselves Christ's righteousness while submitting not to his Spirit do but deceive themselves. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the ashes — the virtue of the sin-offering preserved; the running water — living water, signifying the Spirit; the put together in a vessel — the two composing the cleansing; the blood and Spirit joined — no purifying without both (the merit applied by the Spirit).

The witnesses agree the two together compose the cleansing, and both have their meaning: the ashes of the heifer are the virtue of the sacrifice, the running ("living") water the Spirit; and the Wesleyan reader presses the lesson — that the merit of Christ's blood and the work of his Spirit are joined in true purification, and that to claim the one without submitting to the other is self-deceit, for there is no cleansing but where the blood is applied by the Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse the water of separation: The witnesses note the the ashes: the virtue of the sin-offering preserved. The witnesses note the running water: living water, signifying the Spirit. The witnesses note the put together in a vessel: the two composing the cleansing. The witnesses note the blood and Spirit joined: no purifying without both (the merit applied by the Spirit). The expositors observe the the ashes: the virtue of the sin-offering preserved. The expositors observe the running water: living water, signifying the Spirit. The expositors observe the put together in a vessel: the two composing the cleansing. The expositors observe the blood and Spirit joined: no purifying without both (the merit applied by the Spirit). The believer is taught to mark the the ashes, the running water, the put together in a vessel, the blood and Spirit joined, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that the ashes and running water made the cleansing — the virtue of the sacrifice and the Spirit joined, no true purification where the blood is not applied by the Spirit.
18:
"And a clean person shall take hyssop, and dip it in the water, and sprinkle it upon the tent, and upon all the vessels, and upon the persons that were there..." The witnesses note the clean person taking hyssop, dipping it in the water, and sprinkling the tent, vessels, and persons defiled. The witnesses note that Gill notes three stalks of hyssop bound together, the sprinkler being a clean person, not necessarily a priest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the sprinkling upon the tent, vessels, and persons, and upon him that touched a bone, the slain, the dead, or a grave. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a clean person — the sprinkler free of defilement; the take hyssop — the lowly herb of purging (Ps. 51:7); the dip it in the water — charged with the water of separation; the sprinkle the defiled — the cleansing applied to all death had touched.

The witnesses agree the lowly hyssop was the instrument of cleansing, as in David's prayer: "purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean" (Ps. 51:7); the clean man dipped it and sprinkled the defiled, applying the water to all that death had touched — a figure of the believer brought clean to the unclean, the cleansing of Christ applied by his appointed means to the heart and the whole life that sin had defiled.

So the witnesses make the verse the sprinkling: The witnesses note the a clean person: the sprinkler free of defilement. The witnesses note the take hyssop: the lowly herb of purging (Ps. 51:7). The witnesses note the dip it in the water: charged with the water of separation. The witnesses note the sprinkle the defiled: the cleansing applied to all death had touched. The expositors observe the a clean person: the sprinkler free of defilement. The expositors observe the take hyssop: the lowly herb of purging (Ps. 51:7). The expositors observe the dip it in the water: charged with the water of separation. The expositors observe the sprinkle the defiled: the cleansing applied to all death had touched. The believer is taught to mark the a clean person, the take hyssop, the dip it in the water, the sprinkle the defiled, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that the clean person sprinkled the defiled with hyssop dipped in the water — the lowly herb of purging (Ps. 51:7), the cleansing of Christ applied to all that sin had defiled.
19:
"And the clean person shall sprinkle upon the unclean on the third day, and on the seventh day: and on the seventh day he shall purify himself, and wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and shall be clean at even." The witnesses note the clean sprinkling the unclean on the third and seventh days, who then washed and was clean at even. The witnesses note that Gill notes the clean priest, or any clean person, sprinkled the water of purification upon the unclean. The witnesses note that Ellicott clarifies the sense — the clean person purifies the unclean, who then washes himself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sprinkle on the third and seventh — the appointed applications; the the clean upon the unclean — the cleansing brought from another; the wash and bathe — the man's own use of the cleansing; the clean at even — fully restored to fellowship.

The witnesses agree the cleansing required both the sprinkling from another and the man's own washing: the clean person applied the water, but the unclean must also wash his clothes and bathe, that the cleansing be both received and worked through; the figure joins the imputed and the applied — the blood sprinkled by the appointed means, and the man's own use of the cleansing, that he be "clean at even," fully restored to the fellowship from which death had cut him off.

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing completed: The witnesses note the sprinkle on the third and seventh: the appointed applications. The witnesses note the the clean upon the unclean: the cleansing brought from another. The witnesses note the wash and bathe: the man's own use of the cleansing. The witnesses note the clean at even: fully restored to fellowship. The expositors observe the sprinkle on the third and seventh: the appointed applications. The expositors observe the the clean upon the unclean: the cleansing brought from another. The expositors observe the wash and bathe: the man's own use of the cleansing. The expositors observe the clean at even: fully restored to fellowship. The believer is taught to mark the sprinkle on the third and seventh, the the clean upon the unclean, the wash and bathe, the clean at even, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that the clean sprinkled the unclean and he washed himself, clean at even — the cleansing both received from another and worked through, the defiled fully restored to fellowship.
20:
"But the man that shall be unclean, and shall not purify himself, that soul shall be cut off from among the congregation, because he hath defiled the sanctuary of the LORD..." The witnesses note again the sentence on the man who will not purify himself — cut off, for he has defiled the sanctuary. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of the man unclean by touching any dead body, bone, or grave. The witnesses note that Wesley reads "shall not purify himself" as one who contemptuously refuses to submit to this way of purification. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the shall not purify himself — contemptuously refusing the cleansing; the cut off — the sentence on the despiser; the defiled the sanctuary — drawing near unclean; the no other purifying — to scorn the cleansing is to perish in it (Heb. 10:29).

The witnesses agree the repetition fastens the warning on contempt, not mere failure: the man cut off is one who would not use the cleansing God provided, despising the appointed remedy; the lesson reaches to the gospel — that those who refuse the one cleansing for sin, the blood of Christ, and yet would draw near to God, defile his sanctuary and cut themselves off, for there is no other purifying, and to scorn it is to perish in one's uncleanness (cf. Heb. 10:29).

So the witnesses make the verse the contemptuous cut off: The witnesses note the shall not purify himself: contemptuously refusing the cleansing. The witnesses note the cut off: the sentence on the despiser. The witnesses note the defiled the sanctuary: drawing near unclean. The witnesses note the no other purifying: to scorn the cleansing is to perish in it (Heb. 10:29). The expositors observe the shall not purify himself: contemptuously refusing the cleansing. The expositors observe the cut off: the sentence on the despiser. The expositors observe the defiled the sanctuary: drawing near unclean. The expositors observe the no other purifying: to scorn the cleansing is to perish in it (Heb. 10:29). The believer is taught to mark the shall not purify himself, the cut off, the defiled the sanctuary, the no other purifying, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that the man who will not purify himself is cut off for defiling the sanctuary — the warning fastened on contempt, to scorn the one cleansing being to perish in one's uncleanness (Heb. 10:29).
21:
"And it shall be a perpetual statute unto them, that he that sprinkleth the water of separation shall wash his clothes; and he that toucheth the water of separation shall be unclean until even." The witnesses note the perpetual statute, and the renewed paradox — even the sprinkler of the cleansing water was made unclean by it. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was a perpetual statute to Israel throughout their generations, until the coming of the Messiah, when the ceremonial law of washings and purifications was abolished. The witnesses note that the sprinkler of the water, and he that touched it, were themselves made unclean. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a perpetual statute — binding till the Messiah came; the the sprinkler defiled — made unclean by the cleansing water; the he that toucheth it — unclean until even; the until the reformation — the shadow standing till the substance came (Heb. 9:10).

The witnesses agree the paradox runs to the end of the rite: the very water that cleansed the defiled defiled the clean who handled it, the purifying made its minister impure; the mystery, like the heifer that defiled its preparers, points again to Christ, who in bearing away our uncleanness was himself "made sin," and to the truth that the cleansing of sinners cost the spotless his own reckoning with defilement — and the "perpetual statute" stood only "until the time of reformation" (Heb. 9:10), when the substance came.

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing that defiles: The witnesses note the a perpetual statute: binding till the Messiah came. The witnesses note the the sprinkler defiled: made unclean by the cleansing water. The witnesses note the he that toucheth it: unclean until even. The witnesses note the until the reformation: the shadow standing till the substance came (Heb. 9:10). The expositors observe the a perpetual statute: binding till the Messiah came. The expositors observe the the sprinkler defiled: made unclean by the cleansing water. The expositors observe the he that toucheth it: unclean until even. The expositors observe the until the reformation: the shadow standing till the substance came (Heb. 9:10). The believer is taught to mark the a perpetual statute, the the sprinkler defiled, the he that toucheth it, the until the reformation, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that even the sprinkler of the water was made unclean — the cleansing defiling its minister, pointing again to Christ made sin, the shadow standing only till the reformation came (Heb. 9:10).
22:
"And whatsoever the unclean person toucheth shall be unclean; and the soul that toucheth it shall be unclean until even." The witnesses note the spreading of defilement — whatever the unclean touched became unclean, and whoever touched that. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of the person unclean by touching a dead body, bone, or grave; whatever he touched was unclean, and the soul that touched that, denoting the spreading nature of pollution. The witnesses note that the defilement passed from the unclean to all he handled. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the whatsoever he toucheth — all the unclean handled defiled; the the soul that toucheth it — the pollution passing further; the contagious uncleanness — sin catching and communicative; the shun and be cleansed — the only stop to so spreading a taint.

The witnesses agree the closing verse fixes the great lesson of the chapter — the contagiousness of uncleanness: the unclean man defiled all he touched, and that in turn whoever touched it, the pollution spreading from hand to hand; so sin, which all this defilement figures, is catching and communicative, defiling whatever it touches, and the soul that would be kept clean must both shun the unclean thing and use the cleansing God provides, for nothing else can stop the spread of so contagious a defilement.

So the witnesses make the verse the spreading defilement: The witnesses note the whatsoever he toucheth: all the unclean handled defiled. The witnesses note the the soul that toucheth it: the pollution passing further. The witnesses note the contagious uncleanness: sin catching and communicative. The witnesses note the shun and be cleansed: the only stop to so spreading a taint. The expositors observe the whatsoever he toucheth: all the unclean handled defiled. The expositors observe the the soul that toucheth it: the pollution passing further. The expositors observe the contagious uncleanness: sin catching and communicative. The expositors observe the shun and be cleansed: the only stop to so spreading a taint. The believer is taught to mark the whatsoever he toucheth, the the soul that toucheth it, the contagious uncleanness, the shun and be cleansed, and to use the cleansing God provides. The verse thus records that whatever the unclean touched became unclean, and whoever touched that — the contagiousness of uncleanness, sin catching and communicative, stopped only by shunning it and using God's cleansing.
